coredata.dc:title: Graphene-copper nanocomposites improve fruit quality and the content of bioactive compounds in tomato
coredata.dc:creator: Cota-Ungson, Diana
González-García, Yolanda
Pérez-Alvarez, Marissa
Cadenas-Pliego, Gregorio
Alpuche-Solís, Ángel Gabriel
Juárez-Maldonado, Antonio
coredata.dc:description: Tomato fruits are an important source of bioactive compounds that can positively influence human health, so their consumption is very important in the human diet. Hence, developing technologies and strategies to improve the content of bioactive compounds in tomato fruits becomes essential. The objective of this study was to determine the effect of graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites on the quality of tomato fruits and their content of bioactive compounds. For this, the application to the soil of Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposite (GCHN), only functionalized graphene nanoplatelets (FGNP), and a control (T0) were evaluated. The results showed that the application of GCHN increased the firmness of tomato fruits by 12.6%. Furthermore, it was observed that the nanocomposites increased the carotenoid content, GCHN increased the lycopene content by 18.6%, and the β-carotene content by 95.2%. The glutathione content also increased by the application of GCHN, 19.3% more than T0. No effect was observed for GCHN in the content of phenols and flavonoids; however, FGNP decreased the flavonoids content by 11.1% compared to T0. The antioxidant activity of the lipophilic compounds was increased by GCHN up to 14.8%, while the total antioxidant activity was increased by approximately 6.5%. The content of proline and total amino acids was increased only by FGNP. The application of Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites is effective in increasing the content of bioactive compounds in tomato fruits, so it can be a useful tool to improve the quality of these fruits.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical properties of tomato fruits, firmness (A), total soluble solids (TSS) (B), pH (C) and electric conductivity (D). T0: control. GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites. FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets. n = 6 ± standard error. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments according to Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05).

Lycopene (A) and β-carotene (B) content on tomato fruits. T0: control. GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites. FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets. n = 6 ± standard error. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments according to Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05).

Ascorbic acid (A) and glutathione (B) content on tomato fruits. T0: control. GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites. FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets. n = 6 ± standard error. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments according to Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05).

Flavonoids (A) and phenols (B) content on tomato fruits. T0: control. GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites. FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets. n = 6 ± standard error. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments according to Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05).

Antioxidant activity of hydrophilic compounds (A), lipophilic compounds (B) and total compounds (hydrophilic + lipophilic) (C) on tomato fruits. T0: control. GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites. FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets. n = 6 ± standard error. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments according to Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05).

Content of proline (A), total amino acids (B) and total proteins (C) on tomato fruits. T0: control. GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites. FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets. n = 6 ± standard error. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments according to Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05).

Pearson´s correlation of the response variables (A), Hierarchical cluster analysis (B), and Biplot of the treatments and response variables (C) of tomato fruits. T0: control, GCHN: graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites, FGNP: functionalized graphene nanoplatelets, GSH: glutathione, DPPH-L: antioxidant activity of lipophilic compounds, DPPH-H: antioxidant activity of hydrophilic compounds, H+L: antioxidant activity of lipophilic + hydrophilic compounds, pH: hydrogen potential, TSS: total soluble solids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tomato fruits are an important source of bioactive compounds that can positively influence human health, so their consumption is very important in the human diet. Hence, developing technologies and strategies to improve the content of bioactive compounds in tomato fruits becomes essential. The objective of this study was to determine the effect of graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites on the quality of tomato fruits and their content of bioactive compounds. For this, the application to the soil of Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposite (GCHN), only functionalized graphene nanoplatelets (FGNP), and a control (T0) were evaluated. The results showed that the application of GCHN increased the firmness of tomato fruits by 12.6%. Furthermore, it was observed that the nanocomposites increased the carotenoid content, GCHN increased the lycopene content by 18.6%, and the β-carotene content by 95.2%. The glutathione content also increased by the application of GCHN, 19.3% more than T0. No effect was observed for GCHN in the content of phenols and flavonoids; however, FGNP decreased the flavonoids content by 11.1% compared to T0. The antioxidant activity of the lipophilic compounds was increased by GCHN up to 14.8%, while the total antioxidant activity was increased by approximately 6.5%. The content of proline and total amino acids was increased only by FGNP. The application of Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites is effective in increasing the content of bioactive compounds in tomato fruits, so it can be a useful tool to improve the quality of these fruits.

ce:def-description.ce:para: hybrid nanocomposites of graphene and Cu nanoparticles
copper oxide nanoparticles decorated with reduced graphene oxide nanosheets
antioxidant capacity of hydrophilic plus lipophilic compounds

ce:section.ce:para: Tomato fruits are an important source of nutrients and bioactive compounds that are beneficial for human health (Coelho et al., 2023). Hence, tomato cultivation is not only important due to the crop area that is dedicated to the production of this vegetable, but also because tomato fruits are fundamental for the human diet by providing a series of bioactive compounds such as carotenoids (lycopene, β-carotene), phenols, flavonoids, vitamin C, among others, which positively impact human health (Coelho et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2022). Tomato and its derivatives have antioxidant properties, due to carotenoids and phenolic compounds, which when consumed in the human diet are inversely related to coronary heart disease and different types of cancer; but it also has positive effects on eye disorders, neurodegenerative diseases, immune system, and others (Coelho et al., 2023; Laranjeira et al., 2022). Furthermore, it has been proposed that tomato and its derivatives can be incorporated in functional food formulation programs to reduce the incidence of nutritional diseases, due to the high content of bioactive compounds and minerals (Kaboré et al., 2022).

However, the content of bioactive compounds in tomato fruits is highly variable, and depends on the degree of maturity of the fruits, and even on the tomato varieties themselves (Schmidt-Szantner et al., 2022). Furthermore, the physicochemical properties of the fruits can be affected by different environmental factors that can directly influence the development of the crop, or, due to factors related to the post-harvest handling of the fruits (Guo et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2022). Even the industrial processing of tomato fruits can modify the content of nutrients and bioactive compounds (Wu et al., 2022).

The tomato fruit, due to its characteristic of being climacteric, after harvesting continues with its physiological processes such as ripening, respiration, etc. which can result in loss of firmness and softening of fruits, and ultimately, overall loss of fruit quality. For this reason, it becomes essential to develop technologies that allow improving the quality of the fruits, both their physicochemical characteristics and the content of bioactive compounds. In this sense, nanotechnology can be a tool that can serve as support, particularly through the use of nanomaterials (NMs). NMs can improve the production of antioxidants and other secondary metabolites in plants to protect them from damage caused by reactive species, and improve the nutraceutical quality of crops, which benefits both plant and human health (Juárez-Maldonado, 2023).

Due to the physicochemical characteristics of NMs they function as elicitors of secondary metabolites, however, this also depends on the concentration applied, the form and route of application, and also on the species to which they are applied (Juárez-Maldonado et al., 2021, 2019). In addition, they function as biostimulants, which can improve various crop characteristics, such as fruit yield and productivity, as well as the metabolic and physiological activity of plants by developing cellular defense mechanisms (Jha and Yadav, 2023).

While it is true that there are some methods to improve the quality of tomato fruit, such as irrigation or nutrition management, the use of nanomaterials has a series of additional advantages. It has been shown that the use of carbon based nanomaterials can improve water use efficiency and nutrient absorption. When plant roots are exposed to graphene, there is an increase in the H2O, NH4 + and K+ content of the soil as well as on the graphene surface which can improve its availability (Zhao and Zhu, 2020). The progressive use of graphene has been shown to increase the absorption of nutrients on the surface, which translates into greater absorption of total nitrogen, total phosphorus, and total potassium, which is reflected in a significant increase in plant development (Wang et al., 2023). Graphene has the ability to prevent the evaporation of water from the soil thanks to its hydrophilic functional groups that contain oxygen, which can increase its use efficiency (Zhao et al., 2020). Furthermore, carbon nanomaterials can alter the expression of proteins crucial for stress signaling and water transport in plants, such as the aquaporins PIP1 and PIP2, which are considered crucial membrane proteins for water transport (He et al., 2018; Martinez-Ballesta et al., 2020).

Some carbon-based NMs (CNMs) such as graphene have the ability to stimulate the production of antioxidants (Zhao et al., 2022). It has been shown that they can increase the activity of antioxidant enzymes, resulting in a greater capacity to protect cells against oxidative damage (Vochita et al., 2019). In addition to this, nanomaterials induce an increase in antioxidants in fruits by enhancing the activity of defense mechanisms against oxidative stress, acting as signaling molecules to stimulate the biosynthesis of plant hormones (Xiong et al., 2018). Single-walled nanotubes (SWNTs) have been shown to increase salicylic acid (SA) content in tomato plants (Jordan et al., 2020). Furthermore, the application of graphene oxide (GO) increased the auxin content in the roots of tomato plants, which promoted greater fruit production and accelerated their ripening (Guo et al., 2021).

On the other hand, copper nanoparticles (Cu NPs) increased the activities of catalase (CAT, 2 fold), peroxidase (POX, 85%) and polyphenol oxidase (70%) in pepper plants (Tabatabaee et al., 2021). In rice plants (Oryza sativa var. Jyoti) the application of copper (II) oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) increased the activity of ascorbate peroxidase (APX) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) (Da Costa and Sharma, 2016). In mustard plants (Brassica juncea), the application of CuO NPs (8 mg L−1) increased the chlorophyll content, the net photosynthetic rate, leaf proline content and activity of antioxidant enzymes such as POX, SOD and CAT (Faraz et al., 2022).

Considering the characteristics and potential benefits of graphene and Cu NPs, they can be an excellent option to use in order to improve the quality of the fruits as well as their content of bioactive compounds. Furthermore, by combining both NMs, new properties can be developed that result in better performance when applied to plants (Yáñez‐Sedeño et al., 2019). In addition to this, there is not enough information aimed at the impact that the use of these NMs have on the fruits. Therefore, the objective of the present study was to determine the effect of graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites on the quality of tomato fruits and their content of bioactive compounds.

A single greenhouse tomato crop was established using tomato seeds "El Cid F1″ variety (Harris moran, Davis, CA, USA), of the saladette type and indeterminate growth. The transplant was carried out in 10 L black polyethylene containers in a mixture of peat moss-perlite substrate in a 1:1 ratio. For plant nutrition, Steiner solution was used (Steiner, 1961) and this was applied in different concentrations based on the development stage of the crop, starting at 25 % after transplanting, and reaching 100 % when fruit sets. Three irrigations were applied each day (morning, noon, and afternoon) until 15–20 % drainage was obtained. Plants were managed on a single stem and developed for 14 weeks after transplant (WAT), to obtain fruits of the third cluster.

Multilayer graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) with a diameter of 2 µm, a thickness of 8 to 12 nm and a purity of 97% (Cheap Tubes Inc., Cambridgeport, VT, USA) were used. The functionalization of the graphene nanoplatelets was obtained with 4-aminobutyric acid, following the method described in previous studies (Andrade-Guel et al., 2022). Functionalized graphene nanoplatelets (FGNP) contain a 34.5% modification with respect to non-functionalized GNP. The formation of Cu NPs in the hybrid nanocomposite was carried out following a procedure similar to that previously reported (Jardón-Maximino et al., 2021). 5 g of FGNP were sonicated in 150 mL of distilled water for 20 min, then added to the glass reactor previously degassed with a stream of nitrogen. Next, 4.24 g of CuSO4·5H2O, dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water, were added and mixed with a mechanical stirrer for 15 min at 300 × g. After the time had elapsed, 6.7 mL of hydrazine were added and the mixture was stirred for 1 hour under N2. The product was separated by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and ethanol, and finally dried at 90 °C in a vacuum oven. The Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposite (GCHN) contains 26.7% Cu NPs determined by TGA (Pérez-Alvarez et al., 2021). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis showed spherical shaped particles with average particle sizes of 6.0 nm, while transmission electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs presented some platelet-like morphologies with lengths of 22–97 nm (Cota-Ungson et al., 2023).

The treatments applied were the following: Control (T0), Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposite (GCHN), and only functionalized graphene nanoplatelets (FGNP). Treatments were applied directly to the soil, 10 mL per plant at a concentration of 100 mg L−1 at one-week intervals, starting at the transplant, with a total of 14 applications.

For these analyses, three fruits were selected to create a sample composed of each repetition. Six repetitions per treatment were considered for the analyses. They were washed with distilled water, and it was verified that they did not present damage, were of uniform size, and were in a state of maturity 5 (light red) according to the visual color scale of the USDA (USDA, 1977). The fruits were collected 98 days after transplantation and from the third cluster, where each fruit was from a different plant. For determination of physicochemical parameters, the tomato fruits were processed immediately. For biochemical determinations, the tomato fruits were collected and placed on ice for later storage in deep freezer at −20 °C. These samples were lyophilized and macerated until a fine powder was obtained.

The hydrogen potential (pH) and electric conductivity (EC) were determined in 10 mL of fruit pulps using a digital potentiometer (HI98130, Hanna Instruments, Smithfield, RI, USA). The total soluble solids (TSS) were measured in 10 mL of fruit pulps using a hand held refractometer (ATAGO, Master-100H model, Bellevue, WA, USA). The fruit firmness was determined with a manual penetrometer (Wagner Instruments, FDK 20 model, Greenwich, CT, USA). For this, measurements were taken in three different points of the fruit and the average was obtained.

The contents of lycopene (mg 100 g−1 DW) and β-carotene (mg 100 g−1 DW) were determined according to the method of Nagata and Yamashita (Nagata and Yamashita, 1992). The lyophilized sample (10 mg) was mixed with 2 mL of hexane:acetone (3:2). Subsequently the samples were subjected to an ultrasonic bath for 5 min. They were then centrifuged at 15,000 × g for 10 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was removed and the absorbances were read at 453, 505, 645 and 663 nm, and the obtained values were used in Eqs. (1) and (2).

Ascorbic acid (mg 100 g−1 DW) was determined according to the method of Hung and Yen (Hung and Yen, 2002). 10 mg of lyophilized tissue was extracted with 1 mL of 1% metaphosphoric acid (HPO3) and filtered with Whatman No. 1 filter paper. For quantification, 200 μL of extract was taken and mixed with 1800 μL of 2,6 dichlorophenol indophenol (100 mM), with absorbance measured at 515 nm on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UNICO Spectrophotometer, Model UV2150, Dayton, NJ, USA).

Glutathione (GSH) (mmol 100 g−1 DW) was determined using 5,5-dithio-bis-2 nitrobenzoic acid (DTNB) reaction (Xue et al., 2001). 0.480 mL of the extract, 2.2 mL of sodium dibasic phosphate (Na2HPO4 at 0.32 M), and 0.32 mL of the DTNB dye (1 mM) were placed in a test tube. Then, the suspension was vortexed and read on an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UNICO Spectrophotometer, Model UV2150, Dayton, NJ, USA) at 412 nm using a quartz cell.

The content of total phenols (mg g−1 DW) was obtained according to Yu and Dahlgren (Yu and Dahlgren, 2000). In total, 100 mg of lyophilized tissue was extracted with 1 mL of a water:acetone solution (1:1) and the mixture was homogenized for 30 s. The sample tubes were centrifuged at 17,500 × g for 10 min at 4 °C. Then, 18 μL of the supernatant, 70 μL of the Folin–Ciocalteu reagent, and 175 μL of 20% sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) were placed in a test tube, and 1750 μL of distilled water was added. The samples were placed in a water bath at 45 °C for 30 min. Finally, the reading was taken at a wavelength of 750 nm on the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UNICO Spectrophotometer, Model UV2150, Dayton, NJ, USA). Total phenols were expressed in mg EQ of gallic acid per gram of DW.

The flavonoid content (mg 100 g−1 DW) was determined according to Arvouet-Grand et al. (1994). For the extraction, 20 mg of lyophilized tissue was placed in a test tube to which 2 mL of reactive grade methanol was added, and this was homogenized for 30 s. The mixture was filtered using Whatman No. 1 paper. For the quantification, 1 mL of the extract and 1 mL of 2% methanolic aluminum trichloride (AlCl3) solution were added to a test tube and allowed to stand for 20 min in darkness. The reading was taken at a wavelength of 415 nm on the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UNICO Spectrophotometer, Model UV2150, Dayton, NJ, USA). The results are expressed in mg EQ of quercetin per gram of DW.

The antioxidant capacity was determined using the DPPH (1,1-diphenyl-2-pricrilhydrazil) method (Brand-Williams et al., 1995) for both hydrophilic and lipophilic compounds. The total antioxidant capacity (TAC) was obtained by adding the hydrophilic and lipophilic compounds. The stock solution was prepared by mixing 2.5 mg of the DPPH radical with 100 mL of methanol. After this, 6 μL of extract obtained with phosphate buffer or hexane:acetone was taken, and 234 μL of the diluted DPPH radical was added. Methanol was used as a blank. The decrease in absorbance at 515 nm was measured after 30 min. Hydrophilic compounds were determined using phosphate buffer as the extraction solution, whereas for lipophilic compounds, an hexane:acetone solution was used. The results were expressed as equivalents of ascorbic acid (µmol g−1 DW).

Proline was determined according to the methodology described by Bates et al. (Bates et al., 1973) with some modifications, and expressed as mg g−1 of DW. 50 mg of lyophilized tissue was homogenized with 1000 µL of sulfosalicylic acid (3%), and the sample was centrifuged at 15,000 × g at room temperature for 5 min and filtered on Whatman No. 2 paper. Subsequently, 200 µL of the filtrate was mixed with 200 µL of acid ninhydrin and 200 µL of glacial acetic acid in a test tube for 1 hour at 100 °C, and the reaction was terminated in an ice bath. The reaction mixture was extracted with 1000 µL toluene and mixed vigorously with a test tube stirrer for 20 s. The chromophore containing toluene was aspirated from the aqueous phase and warmed to room temperature, and the absorbance was read at 520 nm using toluene for a blank. To quantify the amount of proline in the samples, an l-proline standard was used.

The total amino acids content (mg g−1 DW) was determined according toMoore and Stein (1948). For the extraction, 50 mg of lyophilized tissue was placed in a placed in a test tube to which 1500 µL of citrate buffer pH 5.0, and this was homogenized for 30 s. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 × g at room temperature for 10 min and filtered on Whatman No. 1 paper. Subsequently 165 µL of the extract, 1500 µL of citrate buffer and 335 µL of acid ninhydrin were added to a test tube for 20 min at 100 °C. The reading was taken at a wavelength of 470 nm on the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UNICO Spectrophotometer, Model UV2150, Dayton, NJ, USA). The results are expressed in mg g−1 DW).

The quantification of total proteins was determined using Bradford's colorimetric technique (Bradford, 1976). In a microplate, 5 µL of the extract and 250 µL of Bradford reagent were placed in each well. They were incubated for 10 min at room temperature (26 °C) and then read at a wavelength of 630 nm on a microplate reader (Allsheng, AMR-100 model, Hangzhou, China). The total proteins were expressed in mg g−1 of DW.

The experiment was established under a randomized complete block design considering six repetitions per treatment. An analysis of variance and a Fisher's least significant difference test (p < 0.05) were performed using InfoStat software (v2020) (Universidad Nacional de Córdoba, Argentina). Moreover, a Pearson´s correlation analysis, Hierarchical cluster analysis, and Principal component analysis were performed using Origin software (v2016). For the Hierarchical cluster analysis of the variables, the group average as cluster method, and Pearson correlation as distance type were used.

The application of nanocomposites modified some of the physicochemical parameters of tomato fruits (Fig. 1 ). The firmness of the fruits increased with the application of the nanocomposites, GCHN induced 12.6% more firmness, while FGNP 23.7% compared to T0 (Fig. 1A). The highest content of total soluble solids was observed in the FGNP treatment; however, it was not different from T0, it was only greater than the GCHN treatment by 8.4% (Fig. 1B). In the pH and electric conductivity of the fruits, no differences were observed between treatments (Fig. 1C and D).

The lycopene content in the fruit increased significantly with the FGNP treatment, with 37.7% more than T0. The GCHN treatment also increased the lycopene content by 18.6% compared to T0 (Fig. 2 A). β-carotene increased significantly only with the GCHN treatment, presenting 95.2% more content than T0 (Fig. 2B).

The highest content of ascorbic acid was observed in the GCHN treatment (6.03 mg g−1), however, it was only different from the FGNP treatment (Fig. 3 A). The glutathione content increased significantly only with the GCHN treatment, which presented 19.3% more than T0 (Fig. 3B).

The application of the FGNP treatment showed a negative effect on the flavonoid content, since it decreased by 11.1% compared to T0 (Fig. 4 A). The phenolic content was not modified by the application of the nanocomposites (Fig. 4B).

The antioxidant activity evaluated by the DPPH radical was significantly modified by the treatments applied (Fig. 5 ). The antioxidant activity of the hydrophilic compounds increased with the application of the FGNP treatment, presenting 7.4% more than T0 (Fig. 5A). In the antioxidant activity of lipophilic compounds, a better effect was observed with the GCHN treatment, which presented 14.8% more antioxidant activity than T0 (Fig. 5B). The total antioxidant activity was increased by the GCHN and FGNP treatments by ≈6.5% more than T0 (Fig. 5C).

The proline content increased significantly with the application of the FGNP treatment, representing 6.5 fold what was observed in T0 (Fig. 6 A). The total amino acid content also increased with the FGNP treatment, 14.7% compared to T0 (Fig. 6B). However, despite the changes observed in the content of proline and total amino acids, these did not impact the content of total proteins, since no differences were observed between treatments (Fig. 6C).

Pearson correlation analysis indicates a positive correlation between lycopene, proline and amino acids, as well as the antioxidant activity of hydrophilic compounds (DPPH-H) and total soluble solids (TSS), this means that if one variable increases its value, the other also does so linearly. The antioxidant activity of lipophilic compounds (DPPH-L) is also positively associated with total antioxidant activity (H+L) (Fig. 7 A). In the Hierarchical cluster analysis, where four groups of variables were observed (Fig. 7B): (1) lycopene, proline, total amino acids, firmness, DPPH-H, TSS and pH; (2) β-carotene, GSH, DPPH-L, H+L and proteins; (3) vitamin C, flavonoids and phenols; and (4) electrical conductivity (EC). This partly confirms the positive relationships observed in Fig. 7A. And is due to the fact that the grouping method of the variables in this analysis was based on the distance type of the Pearson correlation, that is, the variables with the highest positive correlation were grouped together within the same group. Therefore, the variables placed within each of the groups present a positive correlation with each other. The principal components analysis indicates that the amount of information they describe is good, since the first component represents 30.27% and the second component 20.26%. The Biplot shows us the groupings of variables and their relationship with the treatments, this indicates that the treatments that were closer to certain variables had a greater impact on their results (Fig. 7C). The lycopene, proline, total amino acids, firmness, DPPH-H, TSS and pH are directly associated with FGNP treatment. β-carotene, GSH, DPPH-L, H+L and proteins are associated with GCHN treatment. Vitamin C, flavonoids and phenols are associated with control (T0). Electric conductivity is observed isolated from the treatments and variables, as observed in the cluster analysis. Another characteristic that the Biplot shows us is that there are variables that have a negative correlation with each other, these are those that form angles close to 180° with each other. In this way, it is clearly observed that Vitamin C, flavonoids and phenols present a negative correlation with lycopene, proline, total amino acids, firmness, DPPH-H, TSS and pH. For its part, EC presents a negative correlation mainly with β-carotene, GSH and DPPH-L.

From a horticultural point of view, one of the most important processes is the ripening of the fruits, since it confers positive qualities to the fruits (e.g. color, flavor, aroma), which makes them marketable and palatable for human consumption (Posé et al., 2019). In tomatoes, this biological process is complex and involves changes in color, texture, bio-compounds and nutrient content, therefore maintaining or increasing the aforementioned characteristics is a challenge for producers (Kutyrieva-Nowak et al., 2023). The quality of a tomato fruit is given by the variables classified as flavor quality parameters (total soluble solids, soluble sugars, organic acids, sugar/acid content ratio, vitamin C, lycopene and β-carotene), external quality (color, luminosity, chroma, green/red, blue/yellow, hue angle and color index), storage quality (firmness) and pH of the tomato (Lu et al., 2021). Therefore, it is essential to improve these variables through different tools or technologies such as nanotechnology, in order to produce better quality fruits. In this sense, graphene is an excellent option due to its large surface area, chemical and mechanical stability, adaptable adjustment in its surface chemistry and low toxicity, which make it a promising material for the base of agricultural distribution systems (Bhattacharya et al., 2023). These characteristics have the potential to improve the absorption of micro and macronutrients by plants, leading to potential benefits, such as improved germination, growth, seed development, productivity and resistance to plant stress (Wu et al., 2023). In this work, changes were observed in the physicochemical parameters of tomato fruits due to the application of GCHN, especially in firmness and total soluble solids content (Fig. 1).

Particularly, in the market, firmness is the main quality attribute of the fruit, which together with juiciness are the most important textural properties for consumers (Wang et al., 2023). The increase in firmness observed in the fruits due to the application of nanomaterials (Fig. 1) could translate into greater resistance to mechanical damage during handling and transportation, which in turn could reduce postharvest losses and increase profitability for farmers and the intermediaries. The loss of firmness or softening in tomato and other fruits is a typical physiological characteristic of fruit ripening and senescence, and is mainly due to the deterioration or damage of the cuticular membrane, which in turn is related to the production of ethylene as well as the solubilization and degradation of pectin by the action of oxidase enzymes (Kuang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021; Posé et al., 2019). Due to this process, the loss of water, and therefore weight, is induced in an uncontrolled manner, which gives rise to the proliferation of pathogenic microorganisms (Huang et al., 2020). In this work a positive effect of the application of GCHN on firmness was observed, since it induced an increase in this variable compared to the control (Fig. 1A). Some studies have reported that carbon nanomaterials have anti-aging properties and can reduce the loss of firmness in horticultural crops because they suppress the transcription levels of the senescence marker gene BrSAG12, in addition to reducing membrane lipid peroxidation, protecting damage cell membrane structure through the production of antioxidant enzymes and reduce water and weight loss (Kuang et al., 2023). They can also induce the expression of auxin-responsive genes (LOC100281448, LOC100191976, LOC103642166 and PIN5c) (Chen et al., 2021), thereby reducing ethylene production, maintaining firmness and delaying ripening (Su et al., 2015). Additionally, both carbon nanomaterials and copper nanoparticles have excellent antimicrobial properties and both are used to control and prevent fungal and bacterial diseases by preventing or avoiding membrane degradation in different crop plants (Lasso-Robledo et al., 2022).

As one of the quality variables of tomato fruits, total soluble solids indicate the proportion (%) of solids dissolved in a solution, which includes approximately 65% sugars (sucrose and hexoses), 13% acids (citrate and malate) and 12% other minor components (phenols, amino acids, soluble pectin, ascorbic acid and minerals) in the pulp of the tomato fruit (Lu et al., 2021). In this work, the highest concentration of total soluble solids was observed with the application of graphene (Fig. 1B), which may be linked to the properties of the carbon nanomaterials.

The soil-root interface is the main pathway for plants to absorb nutrients, water and other components from the soil. Various studies indicate that nanomaterials can be absorbed by roots, causing different effects on leaves and fruits (Hong et al., 2016). For example, they can improve the water and nutritional status of plants by promoting the expression of aquaporin genes SlPIP2;7, SlPIP2;12 and SlPIP1;7 (Kou et al., 2021a). Moreover, they make the photosynthesis process more efficient by increasing photosynthetic pigments, accelerating the electron transfer rate of the photosystems, and ultimately increasing the sugar content (Wang et al., 2018). Soluble sugars and organic acids are primary products of photosynthesis, they are energy deposits in plants that are necessary for the construction of cellular components, quality indicators and also function as precursors of aromatic compounds and signaling molecules (Vulk et al., 2023). Some studies have reported that both graphene and copper nanoparticles can increase the content of total soluble solids in fruits. González-García et al. (2022), reported an increase in TSS in tomato (S. lycopersicum) fruits with the application of 50 and 500 mg L−1 of graphene oxide applied through seed priming. Park et al. (2020), demonstrated the increase of TSS in watermelon fruits (Citrullus lanatus) by injecting 10,000 μg L−1 of graphene oxide directly into the stem. Kalia et al. (2021) observed the increase of TSS in guava (Psidium guajava) fruits when immersing them in a solution of 25 mg L−1 of Cu NPs.

In the present investigation, it was shown that the application of GCHN increased the content of lycopene, β-carotene, vitamin C and GSH, although particularly lycopene increased to a greater extent only with the application of graphene (Figs. 2 and 3). All of these compounds are non-enzymatic antioxidants in plants, and therefore have the ability to neutralize reactive oxygen species (ROS). Particularly, lycopene is an antioxidant pigment found in tomato fruits and its consumption has been associated with several health benefits, such as reducing the risk of cardiovascular diseases and certain types of cancer (Coelho et al., 2023; Laranjeira et al., 2022). In agricultural activity, the application of nanomaterials to induce an increase in lycopene content could improve the nutritional value of tomatoes and increase their appeal to health-conscious consumers, which could result in higher sales prices in the market.

Nanomaterials can activate the antioxidant defense system of plants because they are highly reactive and induce modifications in cell walls and membranes, causing damage to the membranes and formation of ROS and other reactive species, activating beneficial defense responses by activating the antioxidant defense system and secondary metabolism of plants, resulting in increased production of antioxidants (Juárez-Maldonado et al., 2021).

A higher level of carotenoids in plant cells correlates with greater protection against oxidative stress (Morelli and Rodriguez-Concepcion, 2023). For their part, carbon nanomaterials can increase the content of carotenoids and their derivatives such as violaxanthin, causing a lower cellular oxidation state because β-carotene is oxidized with singlet oxygen and the oxidized products derived from this can have a role in defense signaling thereby further enhancing stress resistance in plants (Velikova et al., 2021). It has been reported in different plant species that by applying both copper and carbon nanomaterials the pigment content can be increased. Subotić et al. (2022), reported that the lycopene content of tomato fruits (S. lycopersicum) can be increased by up to 63% with the application of 200 mg L−1 of fullerene through seed priming. El-Abeid et al. (2020), observed the increase in carotenoid content in pepper (Capsicum annuum) by placing the roots of the plants in a suspension of 100 mg L−1 of reduced graphene oxide nanosheet-decorated copper oxide nanoparticles (rGOCuO). Velikova et al. (2021), demonstrated the increase of carotenoids in pea plants (Pisum sativum) by subjecting them to 100 and 300 mg L−1 of carbon nanotubes through foliar route.

Other antioxidant compounds can be increased with the application of nanomaterials. Kuang et al. (2023), reported an increase in the vitamin C content in turnip leaves (Brassica campestris) when applying 0.5, 1.5, 2, and 5 mg mL−1 of carbon dots via foliar application. Pérez-Labrada et al. (2019), observed an increase in vitamin C and GSH in tomato fruits (S. lycopersicum) with the application of 250 mg L−1 of Cu NPs through foliar route. In this same species, González-García et al. (2022), reported an increase in the antioxidant capacity measured by the DPPH method in fruits with the application through seed priming of 50 and 250 mg L−1 of carbon nanotubes. Ahmad et al. (2020), reported the increase in DPPH with the application of 2 and 20 mg L−1 of Cu NPs added to the Murashige and Skoog (MS) culture medium in candyleaf leaves (Stevia rebaudiana). Antioxidants are compounds that protect cells from damage caused by free radicals, which are unstable molecules that occur naturally in the human body and can cause cellular damage and contribute to aging and various chronic diseases. Additionally, antioxidants can improve the nutritional quality of foods by protecting nutrients from oxidation and prolonging their shelf life. Hence, increasing antioxidants in fruits can have benefits for both human health and the food industry, and can be an effective way to improve the nutritional value and quality of the foods we consume.

The flavonoid content was decreased with the application of graphene, while no significant differences were observed in the phenolic content (Fig. 4). Various studies show that the response in plants for the production of secondary metabolites to the application of nanoparticles or nanomaterials can be positive, negative or null depending on the doses used and the plant species evaluated (Wang et al., 2019). This responses maybe is linked to the fact that the nanomaterials can generate oxidative stress, and although secondary metabolism can also be activated (Juárez-Maldonado et al., 2021), perhaps the stress was so great that it induced the opposite effect. In any case, it is highly recommended to continue studying the possible impacts of these nanocomposites on secondary metabolism.

The proline and total amino acids content were increased significantly with the application of the FGNP treatment (Fig. 6A and B). This is a very important result, given that amino acids are important bioactive compounds in the human diet and are also important molecules to improve plant performance since they are precursors of the biosynthesis of proteins and osmolytes, contribute to the regulation of the transport of ions and cellular enzymes and are essential in nitrogen metabolism to improve the growth and development of plants (Bityutskii et al., 2021). They also improve resistance to stress in plants since they promote the elimination of ROS by inducing the activation of antioxidant enzymes, which helps stabilize the structure of the cell membrane and prevent plant cells from suffering oxidative stress (Wang et al., 2021, 2023). Particularly, proline acts as an osmotic regulator to stabilize the structure of membranes and proteins and maintain cellular functions by scavenging free radicals (Wang et al., 2021). These responses are linked to the fact that the nanomaterials can generate oxidative stress (Juárez-Maldonado et al., 2021), this in turn can cause an increase in the content of amino acids as signaling molecules to activate the antioxidant defense system, or to induce the reprogramming of nitrogen metabolism and manage the growth and development of plants as a method of defense against stress (Bityutskii et al., 2021). Chen et al. (2022), reported that in alfalfa plants (Medicago sativa) treated with 1 and 2% of graphene through seed priming, the content of amino acids increased and especially tryptophan, which served as a precursor for the synthesis of secondary defense metabolites produced as a consequence of the stress generated by graphene. In this same plant species, Chen et al. (2021), demonstrated that applying 1% of graphene in soil increased the amino acid content due to the activation of the genes MTR_4 g055310 (arogenate/prephenate dehydratase), MTR_8 g023140 (alanine aminotransferase), MTR_8 g061360 (tyrosine/nicotianamine family aminotransferase) and MTR_5 g077950 (glutamine synthetase domain protein). In cucumber plants (Cucumis sativus) subjected to 1 mg L−1 of fullerene C60(OH)22–24 in the nutrient solution an increase in amino acids (valine, aminobutyric acid, aminomalonate and histidine) was observed (Bityutskii et al., 2021). Zhao et al. (2017), reported an increase in the amino acid valine, leucine, isoleucine, threonine and tyrosine in C. sativus fruits grown in soil containing 400 and 800 mg kg−1 of Cu NPs. Faraz et al. (2023), observed an increase in the proline content in Brassica juncea plants when applying 2, 4 and 8 mg L−1 Cu NPs through seed priming. Zhao et al. (2016), reported the activation and increase of the metabolism of amino acids (gamma-aminobutyric acid, beta-alanine, glycine, tryptophan, proline, histidine, citrulline, alanine, aspartic acid, asparagine, oxoproline, serine, glutamic acid) lettuce when they applied by foliar way 8.75, 12.9 and 17.5 mg L−1 CuOH NPs.

Some research reports that the application of carbon nanomaterials increases the protein content in different plant species. Kou et al. (2021b), reported an increase in the soluble protein content in Vigna radiata with the application of 0.66 mg mL−1 of carbon dots in the nutrient solution, however in the present investigation no significant differences were observed in the protein content with the application of the treatments. Chen et al. (2021) demonstrated that the application of nanomaterials can increase the amino acid content due to the inhibition of protein biosynthesis or the increase in stress-induced protein degradation due to the application of nanomaterials. In this work, the content of proline and amino acids was increased only by the application of FGNP, but the protein content was not changed.

Consistently, a positive effect of the application of GCHN was observed on the quality of tomato fruits since the application of this nanocomposite improved the firmness of tomato fruits. Additionally, the application of this nanocomposite proved to be effective in improving the content of bioactive compounds in fruits, since it increased the content of carotenoids (lycopene and β-carotene), glutathione, as well as antioxidant activity. In contrast, the content of proline, total amino acids and total proteins was not modified by the application of GCHN; only FGNP application had a positive effect on proline and total amino acid content. This is a clear indicator that the effect of the GCHN is different from the application of FGNP alone, which is surely due to the new properties of GCHN.

The application of Graphene-Cu hybrid nanocomposites can be a useful tool to improve the quality of fruits, as well as increase the content of bioactive compounds, which can potentially positively impact human health. Furthermore, due to the different synthesis methods currently available, as well as the low amounts of nanomaterials required to induce the observed results, the cost of this technology may be economically viable, even more so if all the potential beneficial effects on the quality of the fruits and human health are considered.
